Sorting Algorithms & Techniques

Overview

Sorting is the process of arranging elements in a specific order (ascending or descending). It’s fundamental to many algorithms and data structures, enabling efficient searching, data analysis, and problem-solving.

Key Properties

  • Stability: Maintains relative order of equal elements
  • In-place: Uses O(1) extra space
  • Adaptive: Performs better on partially sorted data
  • When to Use: Data ordering, preprocessing for binary search, finding medians/percentiles

Algorithm Selection Guide

  • Small datasets (n < 50): Insertion Sort
  • General purpose: Quick Sort, Merge Sort
  • Guaranteed O(n log n): Heap Sort, Merge Sort
  • Nearly sorted: Insertion Sort, Bubble Sort
  • Limited range: Counting Sort, Radix Sort

References

  • Neetcode Sort cheatsheet
  • Sorting Visualizations
  • Princeton Algorithms
Sorting Algorithm Time Complexity (Best Case) Time Complexity (Average Case) Time Complexity (Worst Case) Space Complexity
Bubble Sort O(n) O(n²) O(n²) O(1)
Insertion Sort O(n) O(n²) O(n²) O(1)
Selection Sort O(n²) O(n²) O(n²) O(1)
Merge Sort O(n log n) O(n log n) O(n log n) O(n)
Quick Sort O(n log n) O(n log n) O(n²) O(log n)
Heap Sort O(n log n) O(n log n) O(n log n) O(1)
Counting Sort O(n + k) O(n + k) O(n + k) O(k)
Radix Sort O(nk) O(nk) O(nk) O(n + k)
Bucket Sort O(n + k) O(n + k) O(n²) O(n)

Problem Categories

Pattern 1: Custom Comparator Sorting

  • Description: Sort with custom rules or multiple criteria
  • Examples: LC 179, 791, 937, 1029, 1366
  • Pattern: Define comparison function for complex ordering

Pattern 2: Topological Sorting

  • Description: Order elements based on dependencies
  • Examples: LC 207, 210, 269, 310, 1136
  • Pattern: DFS/BFS with in-degree tracking

Pattern 3: Interval Sorting

  • Description: Sort intervals for merging/processing
  • Examples: LC 56, 57, 252, 253, 435
  • Pattern: Sort by start, then process

Pattern 4: K-th Element

  • Description: Find k-th smallest/largest efficiently
  • Examples: LC 215, 347, 378, 658, 973
  • Pattern: Quick Select or Heap

Pattern 5: Bucket/Counting Sort

  • Description: Sort with limited value range
  • Examples: LC 164, 274, 451, 1122, 1636
  • Pattern: Use value as index

Pattern 6: Merge Sort Applications

  • Description: Divide-and-conquer with sorting
  • Examples: LC 23, 148, 315, 327, 493
  • Pattern: Merge sorted sequences

Templates & Algorithms

Algorithm Comparison Table

Algorithm Best Average Worst Space Stable When to Use
Quick Sort O(n log n) O(n log n) O(n²) O(log n) No General purpose
Merge Sort O(n log n) O(n log n) O(n log n) O(n) Yes Stable, guaranteed O(n log n)
Heap Sort O(n log n) O(n log n) O(n log n) O(1) No In-place, guaranteed O(n log n)
Insertion Sort O(n) O(n²) O(n²) O(1) Yes Small or nearly sorted
Counting Sort O(n+k) O(n+k) O(n+k) O(k) Yes Limited range integers
Radix Sort O(nk) O(nk) O(nk) O(n+k) Yes Fixed-width integers

Template 1: Quick Sort

1# Python - Classic Quick Sort
2def quickSort(arr, low=0, high=None):
3    if high is None:
4        high = len(arr) - 1
5    
6    if low < high:
7        # Partition and get pivot index
8        pi = partition(arr, low, high)
9        
10        # Recursively sort left and right
11        quickSort(arr, low, pi - 1)
12        quickSort(arr, pi + 1, high)
13    
14    return arr
15
16def partition(arr, low, high):
17    # Choose rightmost as pivot
18    pivot = arr[high]
19    i = low - 1  # Smaller element index
20    
21    for j in range(low, high):
22        if arr[j] <= pivot:
23            i += 1
24            arr[i], arr[j] = arr[j], arr[i]
25    
26    arr[i + 1], arr[high] = arr[high], arr[i + 1]
27    return i + 1
28
29# 3-way Quick Sort for duplicates
30def quickSort3Way(arr, low=0, high=None):
31    if high is None:
32        high = len(arr) - 1
33    
34    if low < high:
35        lt, gt = partition3Way(arr, low, high)
36        quickSort3Way(arr, low, lt - 1)
37        quickSort3Way(arr, gt + 1, high)
38    
39    return arr
40
41def partition3Way(arr, low, high):
42    pivot = arr[low]
43    i = low
44    lt = low
45    gt = high
46    
47    while i <= gt:
48        if arr[i] < pivot:
49            arr[lt], arr[i] = arr[i], arr[lt]
50            lt += 1
51            i += 1
52        elif arr[i] > pivot:
53            arr[i], arr[gt] = arr[gt], arr[i]
54            gt -= 1
55        else:
56            i += 1
57    
58    return lt, gt

1// Java - Quick Sort
2public void quickSort(int[] arr, int low, int high) {
3    if (low < high) {
4        int pi = partition(arr, low, high);
5        quickSort(arr, low, pi - 1);
6        quickSort(arr, pi + 1, high);
7    }
8}
9
10private int partition(int[] arr, int low, int high) {
11    int pivot = arr[high];
12    int i = low - 1;
13    
14    for (int j = low; j < high; j++) {
15        if (arr[j] <= pivot) {
16            i++;
17            swap(arr, i, j);
18        }
19    }
20    
21    swap(arr, i + 1, high);
22    return i + 1;
23}
24
25private void swap(int[] arr, int i, int j) {
26    int temp = arr[i];
27    arr[i] = arr[j];
28    arr[j] = temp;
29}

Template 2: Merge Sort

1# Python - Merge Sort
2def mergeSort(arr):
3    if len(arr) <= 1:
4        return arr
5    
6    mid = len(arr) // 2
7    left = mergeSort(arr[:mid])
8    right = mergeSort(arr[mid:])
9    
10    return merge(left, right)
11
12def merge(left, right):
13    result = []
14    i = j = 0
15    
16    while i < len(left) and j < len(right):
17        if left[i] <= right[j]:
18            result.append(left[i])
19            i += 1
20        else:
21            result.append(right[j])
22            j += 1
23    
24    result.extend(left[i:])
25    result.extend(right[j:])
26    return result
27
28# In-place merge sort
29def mergeSortInPlace(arr, left=0, right=None):
30    if right is None:
31        right = len(arr) - 1
32    
33    if left < right:
34        mid = (left + right) // 2
35        mergeSortInPlace(arr, left, mid)
36        mergeSortInPlace(arr, mid + 1, right)
37        mergeInPlace(arr, left, mid, right)
38    
39    return arr
40
41def mergeInPlace(arr, left, mid, right):
42    left_arr = arr[left:mid + 1]
43    right_arr = arr[mid + 1:right + 1]
44    
45    i = j = 0
46    k = left
47    
48    while i < len(left_arr) and j < len(right_arr):
49        if left_arr[i] <= right_arr[j]:
50            arr[k] = left_arr[i]
51            i += 1
52        else:
53            arr[k] = right_arr[j]
54            j += 1
55        k += 1
56    
57    while i < len(left_arr):
58        arr[k] = left_arr[i]
59        i += 1
60        k += 1
61    
62    while j < len(right_arr):
63        arr[k] = right_arr[j]
64        j += 1
65        k += 1

1// Java - Merge Sort
2public void mergeSort(int[] arr, int left, int right) {
3    if (left < right) {
4        int mid = left + (right - left) / 2;
5        
6        mergeSort(arr, left, mid);
7        mergeSort(arr, mid + 1, right);
8        
9        merge(arr, left, mid, right);
10    }
11}
12
13private void merge(int[] arr, int left, int mid, int right) {
14    int n1 = mid - left + 1;
15    int n2 = right - mid;
16    
17    int[] leftArr = new int[n1];
18    int[] rightArr = new int[n2];
19    
20    for (int i = 0; i < n1; i++) {
21        leftArr[i] = arr[left + i];
22    }
23    for (int j = 0; j < n2; j++) {
24        rightArr[j] = arr[mid + 1 + j];
25    }
26    
27    int i = 0, j = 0, k = left;
28    
29    while (i < n1 && j < n2) {
30        if (leftArr[i] <= rightArr[j]) {
31            arr[k++] = leftArr[i++];
32        } else {
33            arr[k++] = rightArr[j++];
34        }
35    }
36    
37    while (i < n1) {
38        arr[k++] = leftArr[i++];
39    }
40    
41    while (j < n2) {
42        arr[k++] = rightArr[j++];
43    }
44}

Template 3: Custom Comparator Sorting

1# Python - Custom sorting
2class Solution:
3    def customSort(self, items):
4        # Single key
5        items.sort(key=lambda x: x[0])
6        
7        # Multiple keys
8        items.sort(key=lambda x: (x[0], -x[1], x[2]))
9        
10        # Complex comparison
11        def compare(item):
12            # Return tuple of sort keys
13            if condition:
14                return (0, item.value, item.name)
15            else:
16                return (1, -item.priority, item.id)
17        
18        items.sort(key=compare)
19        
20        # Using functools for traditional comparison
21        from functools import cmp_to_key
22        
23        def compare_func(a, b):
24            if a < b:
25                return -1
26            elif a > b:
27                return 1
28            else:
29                return 0
30        
31        items.sort(key=cmp_to_key(compare_func))
32        
33        return items
34
35# Custom class for sorting
36class CustomComparable:
37    def __init__(self, value, priority):
38        self.value = value
39        self.priority = priority
40    
41    def __lt__(self, other):
42        # Define less than for sorting
43        if self.priority != other.priority:
44            return self.priority > other.priority
45        return self.value < other.value

1// Java - Custom comparator
2public void customSort(List<Item> items) {
3    // Lambda comparator
4    items.sort((a, b) -> a.value - b.value);
5    
6    // Multiple criteria
7    items.sort((a, b) -> {
8        if (a.priority != b.priority) {
9            return b.priority - a.priority;  // Descending
10        }
11        return a.name.compareTo(b.name);    // Ascending
12    });
13    
14    // Using Comparator methods
15    items.sort(Comparator
16        .comparingInt(Item::getPriority).reversed()
17        .thenComparing(Item::getName));
18    
19    // Custom Comparator class
20    items.sort(new Comparator<Item>() {
21        @Override
22        public int compare(Item a, Item b) {
23            // Custom logic
24            return customCompare(a, b);
25        }
26    });
27}
28
29// Comparable interface
30class Item implements Comparable<Item> {
31    int value;
32    String name;
33    
34    @Override
35    public int compareTo(Item other) {
36        if (this.value != other.value) {
37            return this.value - other.value;
38        }
39        return this.name.compareTo(other.name);
40    }
41}

Template 4: Quick Select (K-th Element)

1# Python - Quick Select for k-th smallest
2def quickSelect(arr, k):
3    # Find k-th smallest (0-indexed)
4    return quickSelectHelper(arr, 0, len(arr) - 1, k - 1)
5
6def quickSelectHelper(arr, left, right, k):
7    if left == right:
8        return arr[left]
9    
10    # Random pivot for better average case
11    import random
12    pivot_idx = random.randint(left, right)
13    pivot_idx = partition(arr, left, right, pivot_idx)
14    
15    if k == pivot_idx:
16        return arr[k]
17    elif k < pivot_idx:
18        return quickSelectHelper(arr, left, pivot_idx - 1, k)
19    else:
20        return quickSelectHelper(arr, pivot_idx + 1, right, k)
21
22def partition(arr, left, right, pivot_idx):
23    pivot = arr[pivot_idx]
24    # Move pivot to end
25    arr[pivot_idx], arr[right] = arr[right], arr[pivot_idx]
26    
27    store_idx = left
28    for i in range(left, right):
29        if arr[i] < pivot:
30            arr[store_idx], arr[i] = arr[i], arr[store_idx]
31            store_idx += 1
32    
33    # Move pivot to final position
34    arr[store_idx], arr[right] = arr[right], arr[store_idx]
35    return store_idx

Template 5: Counting Sort

1# Python - Counting Sort
2def countingSort(arr, max_val=None):
3    if not arr:
4        return arr
5    
6    if max_val is None:
7        max_val = max(arr)
8    min_val = min(arr)
9    
10    # Create counting array
11    range_size = max_val - min_val + 1
12    count = [0] * range_size
13    
14    # Count occurrences
15    for num in arr:
16        count[num - min_val] += 1
17    
18    # Reconstruct sorted array
19    idx = 0
20    for i in range(range_size):
21        while count[i] > 0:
22            arr[idx] = i + min_val
23            idx += 1
24            count[i] -= 1
25    
26    return arr
27
28# Stable counting sort
29def stableCountingSort(arr):
30    if not arr:
31        return arr
32    
33    max_val = max(arr)
34    min_val = min(arr)
35    range_size = max_val - min_val + 1
36    
37    count = [0] * range_size
38    output = [0] * len(arr)
39    
40    # Count occurrences
41    for num in arr:
42        count[num - min_val] += 1
43    
44    # Cumulative count
45    for i in range(1, range_size):
46        count[i] += count[i - 1]
47    
48    # Build output array (stable)
49    for i in range(len(arr) - 1, -1, -1):
50        output[count[arr[i] - min_val] - 1] = arr[i]
51        count[arr[i] - min_val] -= 1
52    
53    return output

Template 6: Topological Sort

1# Python - Topological Sort (Kahn's Algorithm)
2def topologicalSort(numNodes, edges):
3    # Build graph and in-degree
4    graph = defaultdict(list)
5    in_degree = [0] * numNodes
6    
7    for u, v in edges:
8        graph[u].append(v)
9        in_degree[v] += 1
10    
11    # Queue for nodes with no dependencies
12    queue = deque([i for i in range(numNodes) if in_degree[i] == 0])
13    result = []
14    
15    while queue:
16        node = queue.popleft()
17        result.append(node)
18        
19        for neighbor in graph[node]:
20            in_degree[neighbor] -= 1
21            if in_degree[neighbor] == 0:
22                queue.append(neighbor)
23    
24    # Check for cycle
25    if len(result) != numNodes:
26        return []  # Cycle detected
27    
28    return result
29
30# DFS-based Topological Sort
31def topologicalSortDFS(numNodes, edges):
32    graph = defaultdict(list)
33    for u, v in edges:
34        graph[u].append(v)
35    
36    visited = set()
37    stack = []
38    
39    def dfs(node):
40        visited.add(node)
41        for neighbor in graph[node]:
42            if neighbor not in visited:
43                dfs(neighbor)
44        stack.append(node)
45    
46    for i in range(numNodes):
47        if i not in visited:
48            dfs(i)
49    
50    return stack[::-1]

Problems by Pattern

Pattern-Based Problem Tables

Custom Comparator Problems

Problem LC # Key Technique Difficulty
Largest Number 179 String comparison Medium
Custom Sort String 791 Character order Medium
Reorder Data in Log Files 937 Multi-key sort Easy
Two City Scheduling 1029 Cost difference Medium
Rank Teams by Votes 1366 Vote counting Medium
Sort Array by Parity 905 Even/odd separation Easy
Relative Sort Array 1122 Custom order Easy

Topological Sort Problems

Problem LC # Key Technique Difficulty
Course Schedule 207 Cycle detection Medium
Course Schedule II 210 Ordering with dependencies Medium
Alien Dictionary 269 Character ordering Hard
Minimum Height Trees 310 Tree centroid Medium
Parallel Courses 1136 Level-based processing Medium
Sequence Reconstruction 444 Unique ordering Medium

Interval Sorting Problems

Problem LC # Key Technique Difficulty
Merge Intervals 56 Sort and merge Medium
Insert Interval 57 Binary search insertion Medium
Meeting Rooms 252 Overlap check Easy
Meeting Rooms II 253 Sweep line Medium
Non-overlapping Intervals 435 Greedy removal Medium
Minimum Number of Arrows 452 Interval intersection Medium

K-th Element Problems

Problem LC # Key Technique Difficulty
Kth Largest Element 215 Quick select Medium
Top K Frequent Elements 347 Bucket sort Medium
Kth Smallest in Matrix 378 Binary search Medium
Find K Closest Elements 658 Two pointers Medium
K Closest Points to Origin 973 Quick select Medium
Kth Largest in Stream 703 Min heap Easy

Counting/Bucket Sort Problems

Problem LC # Key Technique Difficulty
Maximum Gap 164 Bucket sort Hard
H-Index 274 Counting sort Medium
Sort Characters By Frequency 451 Frequency buckets Medium
Relative Sort Array 1122 Counting sort Easy
Sort Array by Frequency 1636 Custom comparator Easy

Merge Sort Application Problems

Problem LC # Key Technique Difficulty
Merge k Sorted Lists 23 K-way merge Hard
Sort List 148 Linked list merge sort Medium
Count of Smaller Numbers 315 Merge sort with count Hard
Count of Range Sum 327 Merge sort Hard
Reverse Pairs 493 Modified merge sort Hard

Pattern Selection Strategy

1Problem Analysis Flowchart:
2
31. Need custom ordering rules?
4   ├── YES → Custom Comparator
5   │         ├── Multiple criteria → Tuple comparison
6   │         └── Complex logic → Comparison function
7   └── NO → Continue to 2
8
92. Dealing with dependencies?
10   ├── YES → Topological Sort
11   │         ├── Detect cycle → Kahn's algorithm
12   │         └── Find ordering → DFS approach
13   └── NO → Continue to 3
14
153. Working with intervals?
16   ├── YES → Sort by start/end
17   │         ├── Merge overlapping → Greedy merge
18   │         └── Find conflicts → Sweep line
19   └── NO → Continue to 4
20
214. Finding k-th element?
22   ├── YES → Quick Select or Heap
23   │         ├── One-time query → Quick select O(n)
24   │         └── Multiple queries → Heap O(n log k)
25   └── NO → Continue to 5
26
275. Limited value range?
28   ├── YES → Counting/Bucket Sort
29   │         ├── Integers → Counting sort
30   │         └── With precision → Bucket sort
31   └── NO → Use standard sorting

Summary & Quick Reference

Complexity Quick Reference

Algorithm Best Case Average Worst Case Space Stable
Quick Sort O(n log n) O(n log n) O(n²) O(log n) No
Merge Sort O(n log n) O(n log n) O(n log n) O(n) Yes
Heap Sort O(n log n) O(n log n) O(n log n) O(1) No
Tim Sort O(n) O(n log n) O(n log n) O(n) Yes
Counting Sort O(n+k) O(n+k) O(n+k) O(k) Yes
Radix Sort O(nk) O(nk) O(nk) O(n+k) Yes

Template Quick Reference

Template Pattern Key Code
Quick Sort Partition pivot; partition; recurse
Merge Sort Divide & merge mid; merge(left, right)
Custom Sort Comparator key=lambda x: criteria
Quick Select K-th element partition until k
Counting Sort Value as index count[val]++
Topological Dependencies in_degree; queue

Common Patterns & Tricks

Python Sorting Tricks

1# Sort with multiple keys
2items.sort(key=lambda x: (x[0], -x[1], x[2]))
3
4# Sort by custom class
5items.sort(key=lambda x: x.priority, reverse=True)
6
7# Stable sort in multiple passes
8items.sort(key=lambda x: x.secondary)  # First
9items.sort(key=lambda x: x.primary)    # Then primary
10
11# In-place vs new list
12arr.sort()           # In-place
13sorted_arr = sorted(arr)  # New list

Java Sorting Tricks

1// Lambda comparator
2Arrays.sort(arr, (a, b) -> a - b);
3
4// Method reference
5Arrays.sort(arr, Integer::compare);
6
7// Comparator chaining
8Arrays.sort(items, Comparator
9    .comparing(Item::getPriority)
10    .thenComparing(Item::getName));
11
12// Reverse order
13Arrays.sort(arr, Collections.reverseOrder());

Problem-Solving Steps

  1. Identify Sorting Need

    • Is sorting necessary?
    • Can we use partial sorting?
    • Do we need stability?

  2. Choose Algorithm

    • Dataset size
    • Value range
    • Memory constraints
    • Stability requirement

  3. Define Comparison

    • Single or multiple keys?
    • Ascending or descending?
    • Special cases handling

  4. Optimize if Needed

    • Quick select for k-th element
    • Counting sort for limited range
    • Bucket sort for uniform distribution

Common Mistakes & Tips

🚫 Common Mistakes:

  • Modifying array during custom comparison
  • Integer overflow in comparator (a - b)
  • Not handling equal elements in comparator
  • Using unstable sort when stability needed
  • O(n²) algorithms for large datasets

✅ Best Practices:

  • Use built-in sort for most cases
  • Prefer Integer.compare() over subtraction
  • Test with duplicates and edge cases
  • Consider partial sorting for k elements
  • Use stable sort for equal element ordering

Interview Tips

  1. Algorithm Choice

    • Start with built-in sort
    • Optimize only if needed
    • Explain time/space trade-offs

  2. Custom Comparator

    • Handle all comparison cases
    • Avoid integer overflow
    • Maintain transitivity

  3. Common Questions

    • “Why Quick Sort over Merge Sort?”
    • “How to make Quick Sort stable?”
    • “When to use Counting Sort?”

  4. Follow-up Optimizations

    • Sort only k elements
    • External sorting for large data
    • Parallel sorting

Advanced Techniques

Hybrid Sorting

  • Tim Sort: Merge + Insertion
  • Intro Sort: Quick + Heap + Insertion
  • Used in Python and Java standard libraries

External Sorting

  • K-way merge for disk-based data
  • Used in databases and big data

Parallel Sorting

  • Divide data among processors
  • Parallel merge or sample sort

Related Topics

  • Heap: Priority queue, k-th element
  • Binary Search: On sorted arrays
  • Divide & Conquer: Merge sort pattern
  • Greedy: Interval scheduling
  • Graph: Topological ordering

LC Example

2-1) Pancake Sorting

1# python
2# LC 969 Pancake Sorting
3# V0
4# IDEA : pankcake sort + while loop
5# IDEA : 3 STEPS
6#   -> step 1) Find the maximum number in arr
7#   -> step 2) Reverse from 0 to max_idx
8#   -> step 3) Reverse whole list
9# https://github.com/yennanliu/CS_basics/blob/master/algorithm/python/pancake_sort.py
10class Solution(object):
11    def pancakeSort(self, arr):
12        """Sort Array with Pancake Sort.
13        :param arr: Collection containing comparable items
14        :return: Collection ordered in ascending order of items
15        Examples:
16        >>> pancake_sort([0, 5, 3, 2, 2])
17        [0, 2, 2, 3, 5]
18        >>> pancake_sort([])
19        []
20        >>> pancake_sort([-2, -5, -45])
21        [-45, -5, -2]
22        """
23        cur = len(arr)
24        res = []
25        while cur > 1:
26            # step 1) Find the maximum number in arr
27            max_idx = arr.index(max(arr[0:cur]))
28            res = res + [max_idx+1, cur] # idx is 1 based
29            # step 2) Reverse from 0 to max_idx
30            #arr = arr[max_idx::-1] + arr[max_idx + 1 : len(arr)] # this is OK as well
31            arr = arr[:max_idx][::-1] + arr[max_idx + 1 : len(arr)]
32            # step 3) Reverse whole list
33            #arr = arr[cur - 1 :: -1] + arr[cur : len(arr)] # this is OK as well
34            #arr = arr[:cur - 1][::-1] + arr[cur : len(arr)] # this is OK as well
35            tmp = arr[::-1]
36            arr = tmp
37            cur -= 1
38        print ("arr = " + str(arr))
39        return res
40
41# V1
42# https://leetcode.com/problems/pancake-sorting/discuss/817978/Python-O(n2)-by-simulation-w-Comment
43# https://leetcode.com/problems/pancake-sorting/discuss/330990/Python
44class Solution:
45    def pancakeSort(self, A):
46
47        res = []
48
49        for x in range(len(A), 1, -1):
50            # Carry out pancake-sort from largest number n to smallest number 1
51
52            # find the index of x
53            i = A.index(x)
54
55            # flip first i+1 elements to put x on A[0]
56            # flip first x elements to put x on A[x-1]
57            # now, x is on its corresponding position A[x-1] on ascending order
58            # 
59            """
60            # array extend
61            In [10]: x = [1,2,3]
62
63            In [11]: x.extend([4])
64
65            In [12]: x
66            Out[12]: [1, 2, 3, 4]
67
68            In [13]: x = [1,2,3]
69
70            In [14]: x = x + [4]
71
72            In [15]: x
73            Out[15]: [1, 2, 3, 4]
74
75            """
76            #res.extend([i + 1, x])
77            res = res + [i + 1, x]
78
79            # update A
80            """
81            https://stackoverflow.com/questions/509211/understanding-slice-notation
82
83            a[::-1]    # all items in the array, reversed
84            a[1::-1]   # the first two items, reversed
85            a[:-3:-1]  # the last two items, reversed
86            a[-3::-1]  # everything except the last two items, reversed
87
88            -> A[:i:-1] : last i items, reversed
89
90            """
91            A = A[:i:-1] + A[:i]
92        #print ("res = " + str(res))
93        return res
94
95# V1
96# IDEA : RECURSIVE
97# https://leetcode.com/problems/pancake-sorting/discuss/553116/My-python-solution
98# https://leetcode.com/problems/pancake-sorting/discuss/274921/PythonDetailed-Explanation-for-This-Problem
99class Solution:
100    def pancakeSort(self, A):
101        pointer = len(A)
102        result = []
103
104        while pointer > 1:
105            idx = A.index(pointer)
106            result.append(idx + 1)
107            A = A[idx::-1] + A[idx + 1:]
108            result.append(pointer)
109            A = A[pointer - 1::-1] + A[pointer:]
110            pointer -= 1
111            
112        return result

1// java
2// aAlgorithm book (labu) p. 347
3
4// record reverse op array
5LinkedList<Integer> res = new LinkedList<>{};
6
7List<Integer> pancakeSort(int[] cakes){
8	sort(cakes, cakes.length);
9	return res;
10}
11
12// order first N pancakes
13void sort(int[] cakes, int n){
14	// base case
15	if (n == 1) return;
16
17	// find max index
18	int maxCake = 0;
19	int maxCakeIndex = 0;
20	for (int i = 0; i < n; i ++){
21		if (cakes[i] > maxCake){
22			maxCakeIndex = i;
23			maxCake = cakes[i];
24		}
25	}
26	// after 1st flip, put max pancake to the 1st layer
27	reverse(cakes, 0, maxCakeIndex);
28	// record this flip
29	res.add(maxCakeIndex+1);
30	// 2nd flip, make max pancake to the bottom (last layer)
31	reverse(cakes, 0, n-1);
32	// record this flop
33	res.add(n);
34	// recursive call : flip the remaining pancakes
35	sort(cakes, n-1);
36}
37
38/** flip arr[i..j] elements */
39void reverse(int[] arr, int i, int j){
40	while (i < j){
41		int tmp = arr[i];
42		arr[i] = arr[j];
43		arr[j] = tmp;
44		i++;
45		j--;
46	}
47}

2-1) Reorder Data in Log Files

1# LC 937. Reorder Data in Log Files
2# V0
3# IDEA : SORT BY KEY
4class Solution:
5    def reorderLogFiles(self, logs):
6        def f(log):
7            id_, rest = log.split(" ", 1)
8            """
9            NOTE !!!
10              2 cases:
11               1) case 1: rest[0].isalpha() => sort by rest, id_
12               2) case 2: rest[0] is digit =>  DO NOTHING (keep original order)
13
14               syntax:
15                 if condition:
16                    return key1, key2, key3 ....
17            """
18            if rest[0].isalpha():
19                return 0, rest, id_
20            else:
21                return 1, None, None
22                #return 100, None, None  # since we need to put Digit-logs behind of Letter-logs, so first key should be ANY DIGIT BIGGER THAN 0 
23
24        logs.sort(key = lambda x : f(x))
25        return logs
26
27# V1
28# IDEA : SORT BY keys
29# https://leetcode.com/problems/reorder-data-in-log-files/solution/
30class Solution:
31    def reorderLogFiles(self, logs):
32
33        def get_key(log):
34            _id, rest = log.split(" ", maxsplit=1)
35            """
36            NOTE !!!
37              2 cases:
38               1) case 1: rest[0].isalpha() => sort by rest, id_
39               2) case 2: rest[0] is digit =>  DO NOTHING (keep original order)
40            """
41            return (0, rest, _id) if rest[0].isalpha() else (1, )
42
43        return sorted(logs, key=get_key)

2-2) Meeting Rooms

1# LC 252. Meeting Rooms
2# V0
3class Solution:
4    def canAttendMeetings(self, intervals):
5        """
6        NOTE this
7        """
8        intervals.sort(key=lambda x: x[0])
9        for i in range(1, len(intervals)):
10            """
11            NOTE this : 
12                -> we compare ntervals[i][0] and ntervals[i-1][1]
13            """
14            if intervals[i][0] < intervals[i-1][1]:
15                return False
16        return True

2-3) Custom Sort String

1# LC 791. Custom Sort String
2# V0
3# IDEA : COUNTER
4from collections import Counter
5class Solution(object):
6    def customSortString(self, order, s):
7        s_map = Counter(s)
8        res = ""
9        for o in order:
10            if o in s_map:
11                res += (o * s_map[o])
12                del s_map[o]
13        for s in s_map:
14            res += s * s_map[s]
15        return res

2-4) Find K Closest Elements

1# LC 658. Find K Closest Elements
2# NOTE : there is also stack, binary search.. approaches
3# V0'
4# IDEA : SORTING
5class Solution:
6    def findClosestElements(self, arr, k, x):
7        # Sort using custom comparator
8        sorted_arr = sorted(arr, key = lambda num: abs(x - num))
9
10        # Only take k elements
11        result = []
12        for i in range(k):
13            result.append(sorted_arr[i])
14        
15        # Sort again to have output in ascending order
16        return sorted(result)

2-5) Largest Number

1# LC 179. Largest Number
2# V0
3# IDEA : Sorting via Custom Comparator
4class compare(str):
5    # __lt__ defines ">" operator in python
6    def __lt__(x, y):
7        return x+y > y+x
8
9class Solution:
10    def largestNumber(self, nums):
11        largest = sorted([str(v) for v in nums], key=compare) 
12        largest = ''.join(largest) 
13        return '0' if largest[0] == '0' else largest

2-6) Permutation in String

1# LC 567 
2# V0
3# IDEA : collections + sliding window
4from collections import Counter
5class Solution(object):
6    def checkInclusion(self, s1, s2):
7        if len(s1) > len(s2):
8            return False   
9        l = 0
10        tmp = ""
11        _s1 = Counter(s1)
12        _s2 = Counter()     
13        for i, item in enumerate(s2):
14            ### NOTE : we need to append new element first, then compare
15            _s2[item] += 1
16            tmp = s2[l:i+1]
17            if _s2 == _s1 and len(tmp) > 0:
18                return True
19            if len(tmp) >= len(s1):
20                _s2[tmp[0]] -= 1
21                if _s2[tmp[0]] == 0:
22                    del _s2[tmp[0]]
23                l += 1
24        return False

1// java
2// LC 567
3// V2
4// IDEA : SORTING
5// https://leetcode.com/problems/permutation-in-string/editorial/
6public boolean checkInclusion_3(String s1, String s2) {
7    s1 = sort(s1);
8    for (int i = 0; i <= s2.length() - s1.length(); i++) {
9        if (s1.equals(sort(s2.substring(i, i + s1.length()))))
10            return true;
11    }
12    return false;
13}
14
15public String sort(String s) {
16    char[] t = s.toCharArray();
17    Arrays.sort(t);
18    return new String(t);
19}

2-7) Car Fleet

1// java
2
3// LC 853. Car Fleet
4
5    // V0
6    // IDEA: pair position and speed, sorting (gpt)
7    /**
8     * IDEA :
9     *
10     * The approach involves sorting the cars by their starting positions
11     * (from farthest to nearest to the target)
12     * and computing their time to reach the target.
13     * We then iterate through these times to count the number of distinct fleets.
14     *
15     *
16     *
17     * Steps in the Code:
18     *  1.  Pair Cars with Their Speeds:
19     *      •   Combine position and speed into a 2D array cars for easier sorting and access.
20     *  2.  Sort Cars by Position Descending:
21     *      •   Use Arrays.sort with a custom comparator to sort cars from farthest to nearest relative to the target.
22     *  3.  Calculate Arrival Times:
23     *      •   Compute the time each car takes to reach the target using the formula:
24     *
25     *  time = (target - position) / speed
26     *
27     *  4.  Count Fleets:
28     *      •   Iterate through the times array:
29     *      •   If the current car’s arrival time is greater than the lastTime (time of the last fleet), it forms a new fleet.
30     *      •   Update lastTime to the current car’s time.
31     *  5.  Return Fleet Count:
32     *      •   The number of distinct times that exceed lastTime corresponds to the number of fleets.
33     *
34     */
35    public int carFleet(int target, int[] position, int[] speed) {
36        int n = position.length;
37        // Pair positions with speeds and `sort by position in descending order`
38        // cars : [position][speed]
39        int[][] cars = new int[n][2];
40        for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
41            cars[i][0] = position[i];
42            cars[i][1] = speed[i];
43        }
44
45        /**
46         * NOTE !!!
47         *
48         *  Sort by position descending (simulate the "car arriving" process
49         */
50        Arrays.sort(cars, (a, b) -> b[0] - a[0]); // Sort by position descending
51
52        // Calculate arrival times
53        double[] times = new double[n];
54        for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
55            times[i] = (double) (target - cars[i][0]) / cars[i][1];
56        }
57
58        // Count fleets
59        int fleets = 0;
60        double lastTime = 0;
61        for (double time : times) {
62            /**
63             *  4.  Count Fleets:
64             *  •   Iterate through the times array:
65             *  •   If the current car’s arrival time is greater than the lastTime (time of the last fleet), it forms a new fleet.
66             *  •   Update lastTime to the current car’s time.
67             */
68            // If current car's time is greater than the last fleet's time, it forms a new fleet
69            if (time > lastTime) {
70                fleets++;
71                lastTime = time;
72            }
73        }
74
75        return fleets;
76    }

2-7) TopK Frequent Words

1// java
2// LC 692
3
4// V0-1
5// IDEA: Sort on map key set
6public List<String> topKFrequent_0_1(String[] words, int k) {
7
8    // IDEA: map sorting
9    HashMap<String, Integer> freq = new HashMap<>();
10    for (int i = 0; i < words.length; i++) {
11        freq.put(words[i], freq.getOrDefault(words[i], 0) + 1);
12    }
13    List<String> res = new ArrayList(freq.keySet());
14
15    /**
16     * NOTE !!!
17     *
18     *  we directly sort over map's keySet
19     *  (with the data val, key that read from map)
20     *
21     *
22     *  example:
23     *
24     *          Collections.sort(res,
25     *                 (w1, w2) -> freq.get(w1).equals(freq.get(w2)) ? w1.compareTo(w2) : freq.get(w2) - freq.get(w1));
26     */
27    Collections.sort(res, (x, y) -> {
28        int valDiff = freq.get(y) - freq.get(x); // sort on `value` bigger number first (decreasing order)
29        if (valDiff == 0){
30            // Sort on `key ` with `lexicographically` order (increasing order)
31            //return y.length() - x.length(); // ?
32            return x.compareTo(y);
33        }
34        return valDiff;
35    });
36
37    // get top K result
38    return res.subList(0, k);
39}
